title: Xi visits Pyongyang and China rehabilitates North Korea
gist: Xi Jinping's June 2026 state visit to Pyongyang marks China's deliberate rehabilitation of North Korea from a near-decade low, driven by alarm over Russia's deepening foothold via the Ukraine war, continued US regional strength through Japan and South Korea, and the prospect of a renewed Trump-Kim summit. Beijing now treats Pyongyang as a strategically indispensable asset it must keep from drifting into Moscow's or Washington's orbit.

insights.claim: China-North Korea relations have swung from fractured to friendly in under ten years, with Xi Jinping's June 2026 Pyongyang trip making him the first PRC leader in forty years to visit North Korea twice in one tenure.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: A multi-decade inflection in the China-NK axis signals Beijing is locking in Pyongyang as a durable bloc partner, raising the geopolitical premium on the Korean Peninsula.
insights.quote: China’s ties with its sole official ally have changed remarkably – from fractured to friendly in less than ten years.
insights.locator: Opening paragraph (Xi Jinping's June 2026 two-day state visit)

insights.claim: Less than a decade ago North Korea routinely conducted nuclear tests and missile launches without notifying Beijing in advance, behavior China deemed unbecoming for a formal ally and that embarrassed its most important patron.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: The depth of the prior rupture shows how far Beijing has chosen to move, underscoring that the current rapprochement is a strategic decision rather than organic warming.
insights.quote: Pyongyang routinely conducted nuclear tests and missile launches without consulting with or even notifying Beijing ahead of time.
insights.locator: The significance of the Xi-Kim summit is best understood...

insights.claim: Chinese state censors blocked netizens in the late 2010s from using the derogatory nickname 'Fatty Kim the Third' as part of a deliberate campaign to rehabilitate Pyongyang's image domestically.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Censorship deployed to reshape public sentiment shows Beijing engineering the rehabilitation top-down, a signal the alliance pivot is durable state policy.
insights.quote: PRC censors in the late 2010s  blocked netizens from using this derogatory name.
insights.locator: As Beijing moved to rehabilitate Pyongyang...

insights.claim: A 2025 opinion poll found 76 percent of Chinese respondents identified North Korea as a friend of China, placing it second only to Russia in public favorability.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Engineered public favorability gives Beijing political cover to deepen ties and mirrors the Russia-NK-China alignment investors track for sanctions and bloc-trade risk.
insights.quote: a 2025 opinion poll finding that 76 percent of respondents identified Pyongyang as friend of China, placing North Korea second only to Russia.
insights.locator: Chinese public sentiment towards North Korea subsequently shifted...

insights.claim: Xi's 2026 Pyongyang trip was his first foreign trip of the year and followed immediately after back-to-back Beijing visits by Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, a deliberate sequencing meant to signal priority.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: The timing telegraphs that Beijing is actively contesting both US and Russian influence over Kim, raising odds of an intensifying great-power scramble on the peninsula.
insights.quote: it followed quickly on the heels of back-to-back visits to Beijing by US President Donald Trump and Russian strongman Vladimir Putin.

insights.claim: Xi traveled to Pyongyang with an entourage including his wife Peng Liyuan and cabinet ministers explicitly to demonstrate respect and give Kim Jong-un 'face.'
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Reading the protocol signals helps gauge how committed Beijing is to the alliance, which feeds Korean Peninsula and Northeast Asia stability assessments.
insights.quote: Xi’s visit to Pyongyang was meant to demonstrate China’s respect for North Korea and give Kim “face.”
insights.locator: Xi needs Kim for Asian regional balance

insights.claim: Beijing sees the Northeast Asian regional balance tilting heavily toward Washington because the US functions as a regional power through its Japan and South Korea alliances despite not being geographically Northeast Asian.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: China's perception of a US-favorable balance frames its NK rapprochement as a counterweight move, a lens for pricing escalation risk across Japan/Korea defense and trade exposure.
insights.quote: Beijing views the regional balance as tilting heavily in favor of Washington.

insights.claim: Despite being an economic basket case with a dubious conventional military and a nuclear arsenal it does not coordinate with Beijing, North Korea remains a strategically indispensable asset for China.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Beijing's willingness to invest in a low-value partner purely for strategic positioning signals it will absorb costs to deny rivals the peninsula.
insights.quote: North Korea remains a strategically indispensable asset for Beijing.

insights.claim: North Korea's support for Russia's war in Ukraine has given Moscow a renewed foothold in Northeast Asia, which Beijing views as unwanted interference in its sphere of influence.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: The Ukraine war is reshaping the China-Russia-NK triangle into open rivalry over Pyongyang, a dynamic that could fracture the perceived authoritarian bloc.
insights.quote: North Korea’s support for Russia’s war against Ukraine has given Moscow a renewed foothold in northeast Asia after years of limited engagement.

insights.claim: North Korea has supplied Russia with munitions and at least ten thousand soldiers, with Putin reciprocating via military technology and money, cemented by the June 2024 North Korea-Russia Treaty on Comprehensive Strategic Partnership.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Concrete arms-and-tech flows mean NK is now an active war supplier, deepening sanctions-evasion and proliferation risk that bleeds into defense and commodity exposures.
insights.quote: Pyongyang has supplied Moscow with desperately needed munitions and at least ten thousand North Korean soldiers – and Russian dictator Vladimir Putin has returned the favor in the form of military technology and money.

insights.claim: China seeks to persuade Kim that Russia is merely a useful friend while Beijing is an irreplaceable ally, leveraging that roughly 90 percent of North Korea's trade is with China including essential petroleum and food imports.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: China's economic chokehold over NK is its core leverage against Russian inroads, a structural constraint that caps how far Pyongyang can pivot to Moscow.
insights.quote: An estimated 90 percent of North Korea’s trade is with China, including essential and sizeable imports of petroleum and food.
insights.locator: But Xi wanted to persuade Kim...

insights.claim: Beijing is driven partly by the remote prospect of another US-North Korea summit given Trump's fascination with Kim Jong-un, recalling that Xi's urgent first-ever Kim meeting in May 2018 was triggered by the impending Singapore Trump-Kim summit.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: A potential Trump-Kim reengagement is a live catalyst that historically forces Beijing into reactive diplomacy, a scenario to watch for sudden peninsula volatility.
insights.quote: Xi’s sudden sense of urgency for a first-ever meeting with Kim in May 2018 was triggered by the impending Trump-Kim summit in Singapore the following month.
insights.locator: A dangerous liability becomes a critical asset

insights.claim: From Beijing's perspective North Korea is geopolitically 'in play,' having shifted from dangerous liability to critical asset, prompting Xi to take action to lock it in.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Framing NK as contested terrain means Beijing will keep escalating engagement, elevating the Korean Peninsula as a flashpoint in US-China-Russia competition.
insights.quote: Pyongyang appears to be very much geopolitically “in play” – and Xi has decided to take action.
